concert.title: Chautauqua Symphony Orchestra — July 9, 2026
concert.subtitle: Toward a New World: Corigliano · Martinů · Dvořák "New World"
concert.notes: Follow along by section. Each section ends with a transition note that tells you what to listen for as the music crosses into the next section. When you hear it, tap Sec › to advance (Sec ‹ to go back). ±5s trims the timer; Pause / Reset as needed. Timings are estimates that drift with the ensemble — you don't need to catch a precise moment, just the broad section changes. Tonight runs about 70 minutes with no intermission listed; short pauses between pieces.

program.title: Midsummer Fanfare
program.note:
Written for exactly this kind of evening: an outdoor summer festival. Chicago's Grant Park Music Festival commissioned it, and Carlos Kalmar led the premiere there on July 16, 2004.

Despite the name, it isn't all fanfare. Corigliano builds it as an introduction and a fanfare: a hazy, fragmented opening that secretly contains the fanfare's notes in disguise, winding up to a frenzied pitch — then the real fanfare cuts through, bright and clean, passed up through the brass.

sections.label: Introduction — the fanfare in disguise
sections.text: Don't expect trumpets at the downbeat. The piece opens ethereal and fragmented — shimmering, unsettled scraps of sound drifting through the orchestra, seemingly going nowhere. The trick is that those scraps already contain the notes of the fanfare to come, scattered and disguised; Corigliano is showing you the materials before he assembles them. Listen for the slow tightening: the texture thickens, the fragments come faster, the tension winds up notch by notch until the sound turns wild and frenzied, pulsing with blurred energy — a swarm rather than a march. Corigliano, born in New York in 1938 and one of America's most decorated living composers (a Pulitzer, an Oscar for The Red Violin), loves this kind of theater: making you wait, then rewarding the wait. The louder and more chaotic this gets, the closer you are to the payoff.
sections.transition: The frenzy peaks — then the true fanfare interrupts it, bright and clean: French horns first with the melody, unmistakably a fanfare at last.

sections.label: The fanfare
sections.text: Out of the chaos the fanfare finally arrives, and after all that haze it sounds scrubbed clean — bright, direct, diatonic, the brass stepping forward the way a fanfare should. Track the melody as it climbs through the brass choir: the French horns state it first, the trombones take it over with more weight, and finally the high trumpets carry it up into brilliance. This is the tune the introduction had been hiding in plain sight, and hearing it assembled at last is the whole point of the piece — the same notes, but now in focus. The writing stays celebratory and outdoorsy, built to ring across an open-air space like this one. It won't linger long; the piece runs only six minutes, and Corigliano has one more gathering gesture before he lets it go.
sections.transition: The fanfare rounds off and the music briefly revisits the hazy opening material — a short recapitulation — before igniting into the final coda.

sections.label: Recapitulation & furious coda
sections.text: A quick backward glance: the misty introductory material returns for a moment, side by side with the fanfare, so you hear the disguise and the reveal one last time. Then Corigliano slams the accelerator — a furious coda, the whole orchestra driving hard, brass blazing, the energy of the frenzied introduction now harnessed to the clean lines of the fanfare, hurtling to the climax that ends the piece. It's a short, exhilarating finish, over almost before you can brace for it. As it lands, the stage resets for something completely different: the orchestra thins out to chamber size, a piano appears in the middle of it, and the CSO's own principal oboe, Jaren Atherholt, steps out front for the Martinů concerto.
sections.transition: A furious drive to the final chord — end of the fanfare. Tap into the Martinů when the oboe concerto begins.

program.title: Concerto for Oboe and Small Orchestra, H. 353
program.note:
Written in 1955 for Jiří Tancibudek, a Czech oboist who had fled communist Czechoslovakia for Australia — one exile writing for another. Tancibudek premiered it in Sydney in 1956; it's now a cornerstone of the oboe repertoire.

The 'small orchestra' includes a prominent piano, which acts almost as a second soloist — a Martinů fingerprint. The music is compact and lyrical: agitation keeps surfacing and keeps being coaxed back into joy. Tonight's soloist is Jaren Atherholt, the CSO's principal oboe.

sections.label: Opening — the oboe sings
sections.text: The concerto opens lyrically: the oboe enters with a singing, long-breathed melody, and right away you'll hear what makes Martinů's sound world distinctive — the piano glinting inside the small orchestra, not as soloist but as color, its bright attacks sparkling against the strings. Martinů, born in a church tower in the Bohemian highlands in 1890, spent most of his life in exile — Paris, then America — and his mature style layers French clarity and jazz-age rhythm over an unmistakably Czech songfulness. All of that is audible in this first page. The oboe line is vocal and warm rather than showy; think of it as a singer, not an acrobat. Fix the easy, flowing character of this opening in your ear — the middle of the movement will deliberately disrupt it, and the ending will win it back.
sections.transition: The song gives way to a quicker, more playful middle stretch — a scherzando episode where the rhythms sharpen and the oboe and orchestra start darting and chattering.

sections.label: Scherzando middle
sections.text: The music quickens into a scherzando episode — lighter on its feet, edgier, the oboe trading quick, chattering figures with the orchestra while the piano's rhythmic snap pushes everything forward. This is the Stravinsky-and-jazz side of Martinů, the composer who spent the 1920s and 30s in Paris soaking up syncopation and motor rhythms. The mood isn't dark, just restless — a game of tag rather than a storm. Listen for how the oboe's writing changes character here: short articulate bursts instead of long song, brilliance instead of warmth. And notice the pattern that governs the whole concerto: whenever the music turns agitated or anxious, the other players seem to coax it gently back toward good humor. That's about to happen — the darting energy will spend itself and the opening's lyricism will return to close the movement.
sections.transition: The chatter winds down and the opening's singing character returns; the movement ends calmly, settling to a quiet close.

sections.label: Calm return & close
sections.text: The playful energy subsides and the movement circles home: the singing tone of the opening returns, the orchestra softens around it, and everything settles toward a calm, unforced close. Martinů doesn't end first movements with a bang here — this is a lyric concerto in miniature, and he lets the music exhale instead. Use this quiet stretch to reset your ear for what's next, because the second movement is the heart of the piece and it begins in an unexpected way: not with the oboe at all, but with the piano stepping briefly into the spotlight before the soloist delivers the concerto's most personal music — including a free, unaccompanied passage that behaves like a spoken soliloquy. The pause between movements will be short; when you hear the music stop and restart slow and inward, you're there.
sections.transition: A calm final cadence, then a short pause. Tap into the second movement when the slow music begins — listen for the piano taking the lead at first.

sections.label: The piano leads, the oboe answers
sections.text: The slow movement — the concerto's emotional core — opens with a surprise: the piano, which has been orchestral seasoning until now, steps forward to lead the music in, somber and spare, before the oboe takes over the song. When the oboe does enter, the tone is inward and confiding, long phrases unspooling over a hushed accompaniment: an exile's slow movement, tender with an undertow of homesickness. Martinů wrote this in New York for a countryman he had never met in person — Tancibudek had smuggled himself out of Czechoslovakia in 1950 — and it's hard not to hear the shared displacement in it. Don't track a form here; the movement moves like unhurried speech, one thought leading to the next. The most striking passage is coming: the orchestra will fall away entirely and leave the oboe alone.
sections.transition: The accompaniment thins and drops away; listen for the oboe left alone — a free, unaccompanied recitative, like a soliloquy, with time itself going elastic.

sections.label: The oboe alone — recitative
sections.text: The orchestra recedes and the oboe is left essentially alone in a free, cadenza-like recitative — the most exposed and personal music in the concerto. The pulse goes elastic; phrases stretch, hesitate, and surge the way a voice does when it's searching for words. This is also where the writing gets famously demanding — long spans without an obvious place to breathe, and reaches into the instrument's highest register that oboists still compare notes about. Watch the soloist here rather than the clock: with no steady beat, a timer means little (nudge ±5s afterward if you like, or just wait and advance by ear). What you're hearing is the concerto's confession, the private center that the outer movements' good humor frames on either side. When other instruments steal back in beneath the oboe, the movement is gathering itself to close.
sections.transition: The orchestra steals back in under the oboe and the song resumes; the movement settles toward a hushed close — then a bright, dancing finale follows.

sections.label: Return & quiet close
sections.text: Voice by voice the orchestra rejoins the soloist, cushioning the oboe's line as the opening's slow song returns and the movement folds itself toward a hushed ending. The darkness never fully wins here — that's the Martinů pattern again, anxiety coaxed back into consolation — but the close is quiet, suspended, more resigned than resolved. Let it land, and then get ready for whiplash: the finale that follows is the concerto's homecoming, a quick dance the dedicatee himself likened to a Czech polka. After two movements of Parisian polish and New York shadow, Martinů finally lets the music go home to Bohemia. It starts with an unmistakable jolt of energy — bright tempo, dancing rhythm, the oboe suddenly nimble and grinning — so you'll have no trouble hearing the seam even if the pause between movements is brief.
sections.transition: A hushed final chord, short pause — then tap into the finale when the quick dance music kicks off.

sections.label: Finale — the polka home
sections.text: The finale bursts in quick and bright — a dancing movement Tancibudek heard as a Czech polka, and once you've been told that, you can't unhear it. After the slow movement's exile shadows, this is Martinů going home: village-band rhythms, tunes that skip and stamp, the piano's bright edge driving the ensemble like a dance-hall upright. The oboe writing turns virtuosic in the cheerful sense — fast tonguing, quick leaps, brilliant figuration — but the show-off element never crowds out the good humor; this is dance first, display second. Listen for the swing between full-tilt dance and brief lyrical backward glances, moments where the songfulness of the earlier movements flickers past before the polka sweeps things up again. It's a short movement — barely three and a half minutes — so enjoy it at face value.
sections.transition: A brief lyrical easing mid-dance, then the polka energy regathers and drives toward the finish.

sections.label: Final drive
sections.text: The dance gathers itself for the last stretch: the tempo firm, the rhythms stamping, oboe and orchestra tossing the tune back and forth as the music drives to a bright, emphatic finish. Notice how compact the whole concerto has been — fifteen minutes, no wasted gesture, every movement doing exactly one thing well. That economy is late Martinů: he wrote this in 1955, four years before his death, when his style had boiled down to essentials. When the final chord lands, applaud the soloist — and then settle in for the evening's main event. The Dvořák that follows is another Czech-in-America story, written sixty years earlier in New York by the composer Martinů revered above all others: the same homesickness, the same New World energy, expanded from fifteen minutes to forty and from chamber scale to full orchestra.
sections.transition: A bright final cadence ends the concerto. After the applause (and re-setup), tap into the Dvořák when the low strings begin, hushed and slow.

program.title: Symphony No. 9 in E Minor, Op. 95, "From the New World"
program.note:
Written in New York in 1893, while Dvořák was directing the National Conservatory — lured from Prague to help America find its own musical voice. He pointed to African American spirituals and Native American song as the raw gold, then wrote this symphony to show what could be built from such material (its themes are his own, colored by what he heard).

The premiere at Carnegie Hall that December was a sensation, and it has never left the repertoire. The famous Largo melody was so beloved that a student later fitted words to it — 'Goin' Home' is drawn from the symphony, not the other way around.

sections.label: Slow introduction
sections.text: The symphony begins in shadow: cellos low and hushed, a brooding phrase that seems to be remembering something, answered by soft winds. Then the stillness is torn — sudden loud stabs from the full orchestra and a growl of timpani, quiet again, another eruption. Dvořák is setting a scene of enormous scale, the calm and the violence both promising that this will be a big-boned, dramatic symphony. He wrote it in a flat on East 17th Street in Manhattan, a Czech villager at the center of the loudest city on earth, and the whole work runs on that double current: New World energy and Old World longing. The introduction is short — under two minutes. When the horns cut loose with a bounding, arpeggiated call, the Allegro has begun and you've met the theme that will haunt all four movements.
sections.transition: The tension snaps: the horns launch a bold, leaping call — the main theme — and the fast movement takes off. That horn tune is the motto of the whole symphony.

sections.label: Allegro — the motto theme
sections.text: The horn theme is the symphony's motto: a bold, syncopated leap-and-answer that will return, transformed, in every one of the four movements — the thread that stitches the whole evening's forty minutes together. Learn it now. The strings seize it, the full orchestra piles on, and the movement charges forward with the rhythmic snap Dvořák absorbed from the music he heard in America: short-long syncopations, pentatonic turns (the five-note scale shared by spirituals and folk musics the world over), drumming energy. One practical note: some conductors repeat this entire opening section before moving on — if the music seems to start over from the horn call rather than relaxing into gentler themes, that's the repeat; just tap Sec ‹ back and ride it around again. Either way, the gentler second landscape isn't far off.
sections.transition: The storm eases and the music softens; a gentler, dance-like minor tune arrives in the winds, and after it a serene flute melody that many ears link to 'Swing Low, Sweet Chariot.'

sections.label: The gentler themes
sections.text: Two contrasting ideas now share the stage. First a slightly melancholy, dancing tune in the winds over rustling strings — folk-flavored, swaying, minor-keyed. Then the movement's most quoted moment: a serene melody floated by the solo flute, whose opening curve so resembles 'Swing Low, Sweet Chariot' that listeners have argued for a century about whether the echo was deliberate. Dvořák insisted he never quoted; he absorbed the spirit of the spirituals — above all from Harry T. Burleigh, the Black baritone and conservatory student who sang them to him by the hour — and wrote his own melodies in their image. Whatever you decide, the flute tune is pure lyric relief after the motto's drive, sunlight in E-flat before the development storms. Hold both new tunes in mind; the development is about to throw them into the grinder with the horn motto.
sections.transition: The lyric calm breaks; the development begins as fragments of the motto and the flute tune are driven through restless keys, the energy turning stormy and combative.

sections.label: Development
sections.text: Now the argument. Dvořák takes the horn motto and the flute melody, splinters them, and drives the fragments hard through shifting, unstable keys — trumpets barking pieces of one tune against strings running with another, the harmony refusing to settle anywhere. This is the standard sonata-form crucible, but Dvořák's version is unusually physical: the syncopated rhythms give the conflict a stamping, athletic quality, less brooding than brawling. Listen especially for the motto theme blared in keys where it doesn't belong — each wrong-key appearance winds the spring tighter, because your ear knows where that tune lives and wants it back home in E minor. The moment of return is easy to catch: the churning suddenly funnels into a full-orchestra re-arrival of the horn call, back on home ground, louder and more emphatic than its first appearance.
sections.transition: The churning funnels home: the recapitulation lands when the horn motto blazes back in full orchestra, unmistakably a homecoming.

sections.label: Recapitulation & coda
sections.text: Home key, familiar faces: the motto returns in force, and the earlier themes file past again — though Dvořák, restless to the end, re-routes them through unexpected keys on the way (the flute tune comes back oddly brightened, sitting somewhere new). The effect is a recap that never goes slack: you get the satisfaction of recognition without the comfort of exact repetition. Then the coda tightens the screws once more, the motto hammered by brass over surging strings, and the movement ends hard and dark, in emphatic E minor — no softening, no consolation yet. That's deliberate: the symphony's warmth is being saved for what comes next. After the final chords, the strings and winds will fall silent and the brass alone will begin a sequence of solemn, otherworldly chords — one of the most famous openings in music.
sections.transition: An emphatic, dark final drive ends the movement. Tap into the Largo when the brass begin their slow, solemn chords.

sections.label: The chorale and the song
sections.text: Seven slow brass chords, strange and hushed, travel from dark to luminous — a door opening between keys, from the first movement's E minor to a glowing D-flat major. Then, over muted strings, the English horn begins the most famous melody Dvořák ever wrote. He chose that instrument — a darker, throatier cousin of the oboe — supposedly because its timbre reminded him of Burleigh's baritone voice. The tune has the shape and soul of a spiritual, though it is Dvořák's own; decades later his student William Arms Fisher fitted words to it, and 'Goin' Home' has been sung at gravesides ever since. Whatever it meant to Dvořák — the prairie, Hiawatha, Bohemia — it lands as pure longing. Let it simply happen to you. The English horn will sing it, the strings will take it up, and time will slow to a walk.
sections.transition: The song fades to a rest; the middle section begins when the key darkens to minor and the flute and oboe take up a more restless, sighing music over trembling strings.

sections.label: Middle — clouds gather
sections.text: The key slips to C-sharp minor and the air changes: flute and oboe trade a plaintive, walking melody over trembling strings — music that paces where the opening sang. Dvořák said parts of this symphony were sketched with Longfellow's 'Song of Hiawatha' in mind, and this stretch is often heard as the forest funeral of Minnehaha: a procession, grief kept moving. Listen deeper in and the mood keeps shifting — a brighter, birdsong-flecked episode rises briefly, winds chirping over pizzicato, like sun through moving branches, before the gravity returns. This whole central span is the Largo's dream sequence, wandering where the outer sections stand still. It ends with a shock, the biggest interruption in the movement: the full orchestra suddenly blazing, and inside that blaze — listen hard — the first movement's horn motto colliding with the Largo's own materials.
sections.transition: The wandering breaks off in a sudden full-orchestra blaze — the first movement's motto theme storming into the slow movement. It passes as quickly as it came.

sections.label: The climax — worlds collide
sections.text: For a few extraordinary bars the symphony's walls come down: the motto theme from the first movement crashes into the Largo at full volume, brass against strings, the symphony's restless public self bursting in on its most private room. Dvořák is knitting the work into one story — these cross-movement returns are the symphony's signature trick, and this is the boldest of them, precisely because the intrusion is so violent against so much stillness. It passes almost immediately, like a door slammed by wind and then held shut. What follows is the return you've been waiting for: the English horn takes up its great melody again, quieter now, more valedictory, the song coming back changed by what interrupted it. From here the movement only descends — dynamics falling, textures thinning — toward one of the most delicate endings in the repertoire.
sections.transition: The blaze passes and the English horn resumes its song, softer than before; the movement begins its long, hushed descent.

sections.label: Return & the dying close
sections.text: The great tune returns and then begins to dissolve. Dvořák strips the orchestra away layer by layer until almost nothing is left: phrases break off mid-thought, the music breathes in fragments, and at the very end the double basses — divided into four solo parts, an almost unheard-of effect — sustain a dark, velvet chord as the movement expires pianissimo. Watch the hall as much as the stage here; this ending makes audiences hold their breath, and the silence after it is part of the piece. The brass chorale that opened the movement returns to close the door it opened. Then the spell breaks fast: the Scherzo that follows starts with an aggressive snap — a quick, hammered figure and pounding timpani, Dvořák tipping his hat to the Scherzo of Beethoven's Ninth. You will not miss the seam.
sections.transition: The music dies to silence on the divided basses and the closing chorale. Tap into the Scherzo when the timpani-driven fast music erupts.

sections.label: Scherzo — the dance ignites
sections.text: Three sharp orchestral cracks, timpani pounding, and the Scherzo ignites — a fierce, stamping dance in E minor whose opening gesture consciously echoes the Scherzo of Beethoven's Ninth (Dvořák premiered this symphony in the same hall season where New York was still measuring everything against Beethoven, and he knew exactly what he was invoking). Dvořák said this movement was suggested by the dance of Pau-Puk-Keewis at Hiawatha's wedding feast — a whirling, show-off dance that spins faster and faster. The music runs on cross-rhythms: triangle chattering, winds hurling the tune across the bar lines, accents landing where your foot doesn't expect them. For all its ferocity it stays a dance, not a battle. Ride the momentum; a gentler, more rustic episode is coming as relief, and its arrival is easy to hear — the hammering simply melts into a swaying wind tune.
sections.transition: The stamping breaks off and melts into a gentler, swaying tune in the winds — sunnier, rustic, like a village dance replacing a war dance.

sections.label: Trio episodes — the village green
sections.text: The middle of the movement swaps ferocity for charm. The winds lead a swaying, sun-lit tune — suddenly we're off the Scherzo's warpath and onto a Bohemian village green, because homesick Dvořák can never keep his native dance music out for long. The movement actually offers two contrasting gentle episodes with returns of the fierce opening music between them, so don't be alarmed when the stamping dance barges back mid-way: that's the design, alternation rather than a single arch. Listen in the sweeter stretches for the giveaway Dvořák fingerprints — drone basses like a bagpipe, birdcall winds, tunes that swing in easy symmetrical phrases you could actually dance to. The pattern to hold onto: gentle episode, fierce return, gentle episode, and then the final return of the Scherzo music, which drives toward a coda with one more cross-movement surprise hidden in it.
sections.transition: After the second gentle episode, the fierce opening music returns for the last time and pushes into the coda — where the horns suddenly recall the first movement's motto theme.

sections.label: Return & coda — the motto again
sections.text: The war-dance returns in full and drives toward the close, and then comes the movement's parting shot: in the coda, the horns and trumpets blaze out the first movement's motto theme yet again, stitched into the Scherzo's final bars — the third movement in a row this idea has muscled into. By now the plan is unmistakable: Dvořák is building toward a finale where all of these accumulated themes will be gathered and settled. The Scherzo ends decisively, and what follows needs no program note to identify: the finale opens with a menacing, rising string figure — like something huge approaching — that explodes into the trumpets-and-horns theme everyone on these grounds has heard somewhere, even if only from a film score it inspired. It's one of the great openings; be ready for it.
sections.transition: The Scherzo hammers to its close. Tap into the finale when the low strings begin their ominous rising figure — the calm-before-the-charge that launches the last movement.

sections.label: The great theme
sections.text: The strings coil upward, step by menacing step — the sound of something enormous gathering speed — and then the horns and trumpets detonate the finale's main theme: blunt, marching, minor-key, one of the most recognizable brass statements in all of music (film composers have been borrowing its DNA for a century; more than one listener hears proto-'Jaws' in that coiling introduction). Allegro con fuoco means 'fast, with fire,' and Dvořák means it. Notice the theme's shape — a defiant call and a hammered answer — because the entire finale is built as a gathering-place: this new theme will spend the movement colliding with everything you've already heard tonight, the motto, the Largo song, the Scherzo dance, as Dvořák calls the whole symphony's cast on stage for the finish. First, though, the finale has its own gentler story to tell.
sections.transition: The march runs its course and eases; the second theme arrives as a long, songful clarinet melody over shimmering strings — lyric warmth after the brass fire.

sections.label: The lyric answer
sections.text: The fire banks and the solo clarinet unfolds the finale's other face: a long, arching, songful melody over trembling strings, as generous as the main theme is blunt. This is the finale's breathing room, and it carries that characteristic Dvořák ache — major-mode warmth with homesickness folded inside it. He wrote to friends that he composed this symphony quickly and happily, yet within two years he had given up America entirely and gone home to Bohemia for good; both truths are audible here. Around the lyric theme you'll also catch quicker, folk-dance scraps — one cheeky little tune gets tossed around that some listeners swear leans on 'Three Blind Mice.' Enjoy the geniality while it lasts. The development ahead is where the whole evening's threads knot together, and it announces itself by turning stormy and crowded with familiar ghosts.
sections.transition: The song darkens and the development begins — listen for old friends: the Largo's melody, the Scherzo's dance, and above all the first movement's motto, all crowding back in.

sections.label: Development — the reunion
sections.text: This is the payoff of the whole symphony's design. Into the development Dvořák summons every principal theme of the night: the finale's march colliding with the first movement's motto; the Largo's great song drifting through in fragments, sped up, slowed down, harmonized anew; the Scherzo's rhythm elbowing in. It's less a battle than a reunion under pressure — four movements' worth of characters discovering they were always members of one family. The craft is dazzling but the effect is simple: everything sounds familiar and nothing sounds settled, which is exactly the tension the coda exists to resolve. Listen for the clearest single moment — the Largo tune sounding against the finale theme, tenderness stacked directly on top of defiance. When the music funnels back into a hard, full-orchestra return of the finale's march, the last stretch has begun.
sections.transition: The reunion funnels home: the finale's main theme returns in force, and the symphony enters its closing drive.

sections.label: Final combination & the last chord
sections.text: The end gathers everything. The march theme returns and drives hard, and in the coda Dvořák stacks his themes in open combination — the finale theme and the first movement's motto sounding together in the brass, the symphony's alpha and omega fused, with the Largo's chorale chords blazing one last time at full volume, transformed from hushed mystery to public proclamation. The music slams toward what promises to be a standard emphatic ending — and then Dvořák plays his final card: the last chord, instead of stopping short, is held by the winds alone and allowed to fade away, a long exhale rather than a full stop, E major light dissolving into the summer night. It's an ending that asks for a beat of silence before the applause. Let it have that beat — then join in for the ovation.
sections.transition: Brass proclamations, the hammered final cadence — and the long, fading wind chord. End of the symphony, and of the concert.
